coredata.dc:description: Strategic design and development of nanomaterials-based detection platforms specific to critical biomarkers like bilirubin holds immense promise for revolutionizing early disease detection. Bilirubin (BR) plays a pivotal role as a biomarker for liver function, making accurate and timely detection of BR crucial for diagnosing and monitoring of liver diseases. In this work, we synthesized blue light emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a single step pyrolysis method, which exhibited excellent photostability and biocompatibility. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence of GQDs was significantly quenched with the successive addition of BR achieving an ultra-low detection limit (38.96 nM) over a concentration range of 0.18 μM–14.29 μM with high selectivity, and rapid response towards free BR. The sensing mechanism was identified as the inner filter effect after extensive investigations. Thereafter, the sensor system was directly applied to human serum and urine samples and was further compared with the conventional Jendrassik and Grof method, yielding satisfactory recoveries. To demonstrate the sensor system's potential for real world applications, we designed and fabricated a prototype point-of-care device (POC) through 3D printing, incorporating paper microfluidic devices and fluorescence image analysis-based android application through smartphone. The compact 3D-printed POC device achieved a detection limit of 114.66 nM for BR detection, proving to be a promising platform for affordable, efficient and rapid BR detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Strategic design and development of nanomaterials-based detection platforms specific to critical biomarkers like bilirubin holds immense promise for revolutionizing early disease detection. Bilirubin (BR) plays a pivotal role as a biomarker for liver function, making accurate and timely detection of BR crucial for diagnosing and monitoring of liver diseases. In this work, we synthesized blue light emitting graphene quantum dots (GQDs) via a single step pyrolysis method, which exhibited excellent photostability and biocompatibility. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence of GQDs was significantly quenched with the successive addition of BR achieving an ultra-low detection limit (38.96 nM) over a concentration range of 0.18 μM–14.29 μM with high selectivity, and rapid response towards free BR. The sensing mechanism was identified as the inner filter effect after extensive investigations. Thereafter, the sensor system was directly applied to human serum and urine samples and was further compared with the conventional Jendrassik and Grof method, yielding satisfactory recoveries. To demonstrate the sensor system's potential for real world applications, we designed and fabricated a prototype point-of-care device (POC) through 3D printing, incorporating paper microfluidic devices and fluorescence image analysis-based android application through smartphone. The compact 3D-printed POC device achieved a detection limit of 114.66 nM for BR detection, proving to be a promising platform for affordable, efficient and rapid BR detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing affordable and rapid analytical systems with enhanced sensitivity, specificity, and durability is crucial for detecting and monitoring medically relevant analytes. Among the crucial analytes in clinical biology, bilirubin (BR) is a key marker for liver function [1]. It is a yellow tetrapyrrole compound produced as a by-product during the natural breakdown of red blood cells (RBCs), subsequently conjugated with glucuronic acid (direct BR) in the liver and excreted through human waste [2]. While BR exists in unconjugated form in the blood (indirect BR) and its concentration rapidly increases during liver ailments, thereby serving as a critical indicator for various life-threatening conditions such as jaundice, hyperbilirubinemia, hemolysis, Dublin-Johnson syndrome, Crigler-Najjar syndrome, and neurotoxicity [3–7]. BR concentration between 0.2 and 1.2 mg/dL (3.4–20 μM) in human blood is widely accepted as the optimal range for healthy individuals. However, it can exceed 2.0 mg/dL (34 μM) in the case of jaundice and may further increase for patients with hyperbilirubinemia [8]. In certain medical conditions, such as acute liver diseases like massive hemolysis, or bile duct obstruction, BR may not be properly processed by the liver and is instead excreted in the urine [9]. Lower serum BR levels have also been reported to elevate the risk of strokes and brain lesions [10],[11]. Therefore, precise detection of unconjugated BR in human serum is crucial, given its role in diagnosing a wide spectrum of medical conditions and its significance as a clear indicator of jaundice.

The conventional approach used in clinical settings for BR detection is the diazo method, which employs sulfanilic acid [12]. Additionally, enzyme-based detection methods have also been explored [13]. However, these established techniques have various drawbacks, including false positives, prolonged testing times, and color fading [14,15]. In addition to these methods, various experimental strategies have been reported for BR detection, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), chemiluminescence, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), piezoelectricity, Raman spectroscopy, and electrochemical methods [16–23]. Many of these methods for BR detection necessitate complex instrumentation, lengthy analysis periods, and skilled operators [24]. These challenges are particularly significant in developing and underdeveloped countries, underscoring the urgent need for rapid, affordable, highly sensitive, and reliable techniques for diagnosing liver diseases through BR detection. Considering these factors, fluorescence sensing has gained considerable popularity over the last decade for detecting biomolecules, toxic chemicals, metal ions, and more. This is due to its exceptional sensitivity, specificity, rapid response, and simplicity [25].

Fluorescent nanomaterials play a crucial role in contemporary research, with widespread applications in the field of medicine, energy, sensors, etc. [26–29]. Among those, numerous reports have focused on developing innovative fluorescent nanomaterials for sensing such as semiconductor quantum dots, carbon nanomaterials, metal nanoclusters, and perovskite quantum dots [30–33]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a subclass of carbon nanomaterials, have garnered substantial attention due to their distinctive advantages, such as excellent biocompatibility, photostability, tunable emission, and water solubility, compared to other nanomaterials [34–41]. GQDs possess a graphene lattice and consist of individual or a small number of graphene sheets, typically with a height of fewer than ten graphene sheets [42]. Notably, the photoluminescence of GQDs can be easily tuned by altering their size and surface chemistry [43]. These characteristics, coupled with abundant carbon sources and straightforward synthesis techniques, establish them as a promising fluorescent probe for detecting medically relevant analytes such as BR [44]. Although numerous studies have utilized GQDs for detecting biomolecules and disease markers, only two studies have substantiated their applicability in BR detection [45,46]. Kumar et al. employed GQDs synthesized through hydrothermal method for BR detection via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), achieving a LoD of 9 μM. Karmakar et al. synthesized GQDs through perchloric acid oxidation using graphite powder as the precursor and successfully applied them for BR detection, where FRET was identified as the mechanism behind fluorescence quenching.

While research on effective BR estimation techniques will continue to expand, the primary focus should be on identifying innovative and optimal techniques for point-of-care BR testing to ensure accurate and rapid results. Miniaturization currently holds paramount importance for the development of simple, cost-effective, and portable BR detection devices suitable for field applications. In this context, Wang. et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor utilizing polymer nanoaggregate and rhodamine B for BR detection [47]. They demonstrated a point of care hydrogel-based assay employing a UV lamp as excitation source, well plates as sample holder and smartphone camera for capturing images, further paving way for smartphone integrated systems for BR detection. To realize such rapid detection systems, it is crucial to miniaturize the platform into portable, lightweight, and custom-designed models capable of accommodating various components and multiple samples. 3D printing, a rapidly advancing technology, has the potential to bridge this gap by developing custom-designed models that seamlessly integrate handheld systems, along with other miniaturized optical and electronic components [48].

In this work, we employed GQDs as a fluorescent probe to detect indirect BR and used 3D printing to develop a prototype device which upon integrated with a smartphone, can establish BR detection in a point-of-care setting. GQDs were synthesized via a single-step pyrolysis technique, demonstrating excellent photostability, low cytotoxicity, and a robust sensory response towards BR. Our investigations reveal that BR effectively quenches the fluorescence of the synthesized GQDs through the inner filter effect, achieving a detection limit of 38.96 nM. The developed sensor was evaluated in human serum samples, yielding satisfactory results and good recovery rates. Finally, to establish the applicability of the sensor system towards point-of-care detection, a 3D-printed fluorescence image analysis based POC device was developed. The device was tested with various concentrations of BR on paper substrates, achieving a detection limit of 114.66 nM. The developed 3D-printed system was integrated with image analysis-based system through smartphone application, offering high sensitivity, rapid analysis, and affordability, compared to the conventional methods.
The chemicals, reagents, and analytes used in this work are provided in the supplementary information file (ESI). Detailed information regarding the instruments utilized is also available in the ESI.

Water-soluble GQDs were synthesized following a previously reported one-step thermal pyrolysis method using CA, with minor modifications as shown in Scheme 1 [49]. Initially, 1.9 g CA was heated on a hot plate at 200 °C for 12 min. The color of the melted liquid turned from transparent to orange during the heating, indicating the formation of GQDs. The resulting orangish solution was then slowly added, drop by drop, into a freshly prepared 50 mL NaOH solution (10 mg/mL). The mixture was then stirred for 1 h while maintaining the pH at 7.0. The solution was then filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter and subsequently dialyzed using a 3 kDa dialysis bag for 48 h. The resultant solution was stored at 4 °C until further characterization.

All spectroscopic studies were conducted using a quartz cell of path length 10 mm. Since BR is not readily soluble in water, BR stock solution was prepared by adding 1 mg BR in 15 mL of DI water along with a few drops of 0.1 M of NaOH. A volume of 200 μL of the synthesized GQD stock solution was introduced into the cell, followed by varying concentrations of BR, and then filled up with 2.5 mL PBS (pH 7) as the primary solvent. The mixture was thoroughly homogenized at room temperature. Subsequently, fluorescence measurements were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (source: 150 W Xenon lamp) and an emission wavelength of 461 nm (detector: photo multiplier tube) at a scan rate of 600 nm/min. Similar investigations were carried out to explore the response of GQDs to other analytes like creatinine (Cre), dopamine (Dop), glucose (Glu), urea (Ure), l-cysteine (L-cy), l-glutathione (L-Gl), fructose (Fru), bovine serum albumin (BSA), hemoglobin (Hem), uric acid (UrA), aspartic acid (AsA), l-arginine (L-ar), l-tyrosine (L-ty), aspartic acid (ApA) and sucrose (Suc). In addition to these biomolecules, a wide range of cations and anions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2−, were also tested for response to GQDs.

Human embryonic lung epithelial cell line (L-132) was purchased from the National Center for Cell Sciences (NCCS), India. Cells were cultured in DMEM (High glucose) medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA) and 1 % antibiotic/antimycotic solution, maintained at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Phosphate-buffered saline (Himedia, India) was used for washing and Trypsin-EDTA (Gibco, USA) was applied for sub-culturing the cells. The cytotoxic effects of GQDs were assessed using MTT assay [50]. In this assay, approximately 5 × 103 L-132 cells were seeded into each well of a 96-well tissue culture plate. After a 24-h incubation period, the cells were treated with GQDs of varying concentrations and incubated at 37 °C. Following the incubation, 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL stock) was added to each well containing 100 μL of media under dark conditions, and the plates were incubated for another 4 h. Afterward, the medium was carefully removed, and the formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 % dimethyl sulfoxide. The absorbance (OD) was measured at 570 nm, and the assay was repeated three times. The cell viability was determined using the following formula:
%

Human blood and urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers at a laboratory clinic in Roorkee, India. Initially, the BR levels in blood serum were determined using the conventional Jendrassik and Grof method for comparison with the proposed method. The obtained samples were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min after which the supernatant was carefully transferred to clean tubes and immediately stored at 4 °C until further analysis. The real sample assay was performed on the same day as blood collection to avoid sampling errors. Subsequently, the blood serum was diluted 100-fold using PBS buffer solution, after which 500 μL of the sample was added in 2.5 mL PBS buffer along with 200 μL of GQD solution. The resulting solution was thoroughly mixed at room temperature, followed by spectroscopic measurements. Similarly, urine sample with known trace BR concentration underwent the same preprocessing steps and were spiked with BR concentrations of 4 μM, 8 μM and 12 μM. Afterward, the urine sample was subjected to 100-fold dilution with PBS, and spectroscopic measurements were performed by adding 500 μL of sample to 2.5 mL of PBS, along with 200 μL of GQD solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of 3D-printed smartphone integrated POC device for BR detection
ce:section.ce:para: To develop a compact and affordable point of care BR detection platform, we utilized 3D printing technology to fabricate a smartphone-integrated fluorescence imaging analysis device that incorporates paper devices as substrates. The development of POC device mainly involves three key stages: (i) the design and fabrication of 3D-printed device platform, (ii) fabrication of paper microfluidic devices via 3D printing and PDMS stamping, and (iii) development of a Python-based android smartphone application called “Fluoroimage” for predicting BR concentration through image analysis. The 3D-printed device platform is equipped with a SMD LED for excitation, an optical filter to eliminate excitation light during image capture and a fidget spinner with six heads to accommodate six paper devices at a time. The device also includes a knob to rotate the fidget spinner, allowing for rapid analysis of all six samples. Additionally, the 3D-printed device was further spray-coated with black paint to prevent any reflection or interference from light inside the platform during the sampling process. To develop paper microfluidic devices as test strips, stamping device was coated with PDMS (1:10 ratio of master to cross linker), and stamped onto the Whatman filter paper no. 1. The stamp transferred the PDMS onto the filter paper, successfully fabricating circular active regions with PDMS surrounding them as the hydrophobic barriers to confine the fluid flow. The filter paper was placed in an oven at 70 °C for 20 min to cure the PDMS, forming paper test trips, which were then cut into individual strips for device operation. A Python language-based smartphone application named “Fluoroimage” was developed to process the images of the sample as well as the calculation of BR concentration. Fluoroimage extracts the corresponding RGB values from each image and further converts it into respective grayscale values which was considered for quantifying BR concentration. To enhance accuracy and eliminate errors from edge effects, Fluoroimage selectively crops a uniform region from all images for analysis.

The synthesized GQDs were first examined to determine their structure, size, and morphology using multiple characterization techniques. The XRD pattern of GQD (Fig. S1a) exhibited a broad peak centered at 23.50°, attributed to 002 plane, confirming the formation of graphite like structure as previously reported [51]. For a detailed examination of the morphology and size of the GQDs, HR-TEM was conducted, revealing (Fig. 1 a) a size profile ranging from 9 to 16 nm, with an average size of 12.56 ± 1.56 nm (inset image at the bottom of Fig. 1a), analyzed using Image J software. A high-resolution TEM image of a single GQD (Fig. 1b) revealed a lattice spacing of approximately 0.34 nm, corresponding to the 002 plane of graphite, supporting the findings from XRD. The selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S1b) of the scan area confirmed the crystalline nature of the GQDs. Additionally, the live fast Fourier transform (FFT) image of the high-resolution TEM image of single GQD is shown in Fig. S1c. To gain further insights into the size distribution and correlate it with the HR-TEM analysis, FE-SEM was conducted to characterize GQDs at a magnification of 128,000× (Fig. 1c). The FE-SEM image shows nanosized particles, with the size profile (inset image in Fig. 1b) indicating an average size of 13.45 ± 1.73 nm. The hydrodynamic size distribution of GQDs were also analyzed through dynamic light scattering (DLS) study, where the hydrodynamic size of the GQDs were found to be 11.71 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. S2a). All these characterizations confirm the formation of GQDs, with an average size ranging from 11.5 nm to 13.5 nm. AFM was performed for a more detailed examination of the topographic morphology of the synthesized GQDs.

AFM is considered an ideal approach for determining the thickness of low dimensional materials. Fig. S2b illustrates the AFM image of the GQDs, along with the 3D topographical image (Fig. S2c) revealing a height profile with a maximum height of 2.4 nm, confirming the formation of GQDs composing of 4–5 layers of graphene [52]. Raman spectroscopy, a widely used characterization technique, is commonly employed to assess the structure and detect defects in carbon nanomaterials. Fig. S2d shows the Raman spectra of the GQDs, with the D and G bands at 1335 cm−1 and 1574 cm−1, corresponding to defect sites in the carbon network and the sp2 hybridized carbon framework [52].

FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to investigate the functional groups present in the GQDs. Fig. 1d shows the FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs, with a broad, intense peak at 3360 cm−1, attributed to –OH stretching. This indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups and confirms the hydrophilic nature of the GQDs [53]. The absorption peak at 2950 cm−1 corresponds to C–H stretching, while the bands at 1556 cm−1 and 1369 cm−1 indicate the symmetric and anti-symmetric stretching of carboxylic groups [53,54]. Additionally, C–OH stretching is observed at 1133 cm−1, out of plane –OH bending at 667 cm−1, and C–O–C stretching at 1077 cm−1 [53–55]. To evaluate the chemical composition and states of the GQDs, XPS analysis was performed and correlated with the FT-IR results. As shown in Fig. 1e, the XPS survey spectra of the GQDs shows three predominant peaks at binding energies of approximately 284.6 eV, 531 eV and 496.1 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s and Na KLL, respectively [56]. The Na KLL peak arises here from the use of NaOH during GQD synthesis [56]. The C1s spectrum is further deconvoluted to three peaks (Fig. 1f), attributed to CC/C–C, CO, and O–CO at 284.5 eV, 285.3 eV and 288.2 eV [57–59]. Similarly, the O1s spectrum comprises of three peaks (Fig. 1g) at 531.1 eV, 532.2 eV and 535.5 eV, corresponding to CO, C–OH and O–COH bonds, respectively [59–61]. The elemental composition of GQDs was estimated from XPS analysis and found to have 78.92 % carbon and 21.08 % oxygen. The information obtained from XPS analysis aligns well with the FT-IR spectroscopy results, thereby confirming the formation of GQDs and functional groups attached to it.

The photophysical properties of GQDs were explored using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1h shows the absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra of GQDs, with a prominent absorption peak centered at 355 nm, attributed to the n-π∗ transition of CO. Meanwhile, the GQDs exhibit strong PL emission, peaking at 461 nm under excitation of 340 nm. The GQDs appear pale yellow under ambient light and emit blue under UV lamp (inset of Fig. 1h). Notably, the GQDs exhibit excitation-independent PL emission with a maximum at 461 nm across excitation wavelengths from 330 nm to 390 nm, indicating the size uniformity of the synthesized GQDs (Fig. 1i). Fig. S3a shows the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram from the fluorescence spectra of GQDs under 340 nm excitation, with chromaticity co-ordinates of (0.16, 0.19). The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of GQDs was determined using the relative quantum yield method, yielding a QY of 12.7 % in DI water at 340 nm excitation, with quinine sulfate (QY = 54.4 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference (Fig. S3b). Additional details regarding the QY calculation are provided in the ESI.

The photostability of fluorescent probe is crucial for their applicability as a probe under diverse conditions. Photostability studies on the GQDs were conducted by examining their fluorescence response under varying pH levels, ionic concentrations, continuous UV light exposure, long-term storage, and increasing temperature. The fluorescence signals varied slightly within the pH range of 6–12, as depicted in Fig. S4a, and quenched significantly under acidic conditions (pH < 6). This observation aligns with the FT-IR analysis, which identified surface functional groups, such as carboxyl groups on the GQDs. Under high acidic conditions, the carboxyl group gets degraded, resulting in the quenching of PL emission. Nevertheless, GQDs demonstrated exceptional stability under neutral conditions, making them a promising candidate for BR sensing. Additionally, the GQDs were evaluated for their PL response in highly concentrated NaCl solutions (up to 2 Molar), displaying remarkable stability with minimal variation in PL signals as the NaCl concentration increased (Fig. S4b). Photobleaching of GQDs was assessed by recording their PL intensity under continuous excitation (150 W Xenon lamp) at 340 nm for 2 h, showing only an 8 % reduction in PL signals (Fig. S4c). The long-term stability of the GQDs was investigated by monitoring the PL spectra over a 180-day storage period, retaining 95 % of initial PL signals (Fig. S4d). The stability of the GQDs was further tested by gradually increasing the solvent temperature. PL spectra of GQDs were recorded as the solvent temperature varied from 30 °C to 65 °C (Fig. S4e). The GQDs exhibited excellent PL stability under varying temperatures, with only an 8 % reduction when the solvent reaches 65 °C. These observations collectively underscore the exceptional photostability of the GQDs under various conditions, affirming their applicability as fluorescent probe for BR sensing.

To achieve the optimal sensor system performance, several critical parameters influencing BR detection, such as pH, GQD concentration, and reaction time, were optimized. The BR concentration was fixed at 6 μM, and 200 μL of GQD stock solution was used for all optimization experiments. PL response of the sensor system towards varying solvent pH was studied. We observed that the PL of the GQDs varied slightly between pH 6 and 12 (Fig. S4a), with significant deviations occurring below pH 6. To determine the optimal pH for BR detection using GQDs, the GQDs were dispersed in DI water with pH values ranging from 4 to 12, and the fluorescence response was measured with and without BR addition. As shown in Fig. S5a, the PL response of GQDs towards BR is pH sensitive, with the I/Io ratio reaching its maximum at pH 7, indicating that this is the optimal pH for BR sensing. Here, Io and I represent the PL intensity of the GQD system before and after addition of BR, respectively. GQD concentration is another crucial factor affecting the sensor system's performance. Fig. S5b illustrates the PL response of the GQDs towards BR over a concentration range of 0.2 mg/mL to 1.8 mg/mL. The sensor response increases with GQD concentration, reaching maximum at 1 mg/mL, before decreasing sharply. Therefore, the optimal GQD concentration for BR detection was determined to be 1 mg/mL. Finally, to establish the optimal reaction time, the PL response of GQDs after BR addition was recorded every minute demonstrating the sensor system's rapid response compared to conventional clinical BR detection techniques (Fig. S5c). Consequently, the optimal conditions for BR detection were determined to be pH 7, GQD concentration of 1 mg/mL, and a reaction time of 1 min. These conditions were used in subsequent detection experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of BR using GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The analytical performance of the GQDs for BR detection was explored using fluorescence spectroscopy under optimized conditions. The fluorescence of the GQDs were effectively suppressed upon the addition of varying concentrations of BR, ranging from 0.018 μM to 14.29 μM, at room temperature as depicted in Fig. 2 a. The fluorescence response of GQDs was represented by generating Stern-Volmer plot, where the BR concentration was plotted on the x-axis and (Io/I) - 1 on the y-axis (Fig. 2b). Here, Io represents the PL intensity of GQDs before the addition of BR, and I represent the PL intensity of the GQDs after the addition of varying concentrations of BR. The mathematical relation is defined as:
I o
I
= 1 +
[ Q ]
K
s v
Here [Q] denotes the concentration of BR and K
sv stands for stern-volmer constant, a parameter that quantifies the sensitivity of the sensor system. Fig. 2b illustrates the Ksv plot, showing the linear relationship of GQD emission with BR concentration, described by the linear fit equation Io/I – 1 = 0.264 [Q] – 0.00437. The estimated Ksv value from the plot was determined to be 2.65 × 105 M−1. Fig. S6a presents the PL response of GQDs across the entire BR concentration range used in the PL quenching study, along with the calibration plot showing the relationship between PL intensity of the GQDs and BR at lower concentration (Fig. S6b). The LoD of the sensor system was calculated using the standard protocol (detailed in ESI), employing the relation 3σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation of ten blank PL measurement of GQDs and K denotes the slope of calibration plot, yielding LoD of 38.96 nM.

To explore the mechanism underlying BR detection using GQDs, detailed investigations were conducted. Significant spectral overlap between BR absorption spectra and GQD emission spectra (Fig. 3 a) suggests the possibility of multiple mechanisms, including Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), ground state complexation and inner filter effect (IFE). Fluorescence quenching can generally be categorized as either static or dynamic, depending on the mechanism involved [62]. Static quenching arises from the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between the fluorophore and analyte responsible for quenching. Whereas dynamic quenching occurs due to collisional interactions between the fluorophore and the analyte while the fluorophore is in excited state. FRET and PET fall under dynamic quenching, where FRET occurs through non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling between the fluorophore and analyte when they are very close to each other and PET induces fluorescence quenching through electron transfer from the excited state of the fluorophore to the analyte [62–64]. Both FRET and PET are distance-dependent phenomena, reliant on the proximity between the fluorophore and analyte (typically less than 10 nm). IFE doesn't belong to either static or dynamic quenching, occurs when there is substantial overlap between the absorption spectra of analyte and emission spectra of fluorophore, without direct interaction between them [65]. A commonly reported scenario is when the analyte, upon introduction to the system containing fluorophore, absorbs the photons emitted by the excited fluorophore before they reach the photodetector, leading to fluorescence quenching, commonly known as secondary inner filter effect. Whereas ground state complexation can induce fluorescence quenching when the fluorophore forms a non-fluorescent complex with the analyte in the ground state, without the involvement of electron or energy transfer between them [66].

Based on these insights, we initially conducted a time-resolved photoluminescence study of GQDs using time corelated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique to determine the fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 3b illustrates the fluorescence decay of GQDs before and after the addition of BR. The observed fluorescence decay was accurately fitted using a tri exponential decay function, resulting in an average fluorescence lifetime of 4.48 ns for the GQDs. After adding 20 μM of BR to the GQD system, the measurement was repeated, showing a minimal change in fluorescence lifetime from 4.48 ns to 4.43 ns, (Table S1, ESI). Detailed explanations of the tri exponential function and fluorescence lifetime calculations are provided in the ESI. The negligible change in fluorescence lifetime may be attributed to the static quenching process between GQDs and BR. To gain further insight into the interaction between GQDs and BR, zeta potential study was conducted. As depicted in Fig. 3c, both GQDs (−32.3 mV) and BR (−19.3 mV) exhibit high negative potential, restricting proximity between GQDs and BR, thereby reducing the likelihood of FRET, PET or electrostatic interactions. In addition to the zeta potential study, FT-IR analysis was conducted to investigate the interaction between GQDs and BR. Fig. 3d shows the FT-IR spectra of GQDs along with the mixture of GQD and BR (GQD + BR). It is evident from FT-IR results that there is no shift in the respective band positions of GQDs after the addition of BR (GQD + BR), and no new bands are observed apart from some bands originating from BR, as highlighted in Fig. S7. This characterization further supports the findings from zeta potential study, indicating the absence of direct interaction between GQDs and BR. To explore the formation of ground state complex between GQDs and BR, UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs were recorded with increasing concentration of BR. As illustrated in Fig. S8a, with the incremental addition of BR, only the characteristic absorption peak of BR becomes more intensified without the emergence of any new peaks, thereby excluding the possibility of static quenching as the mechanism responsible for the fluorescence quenching of GQDs. These observations suggest that IFE is the likely sensing mechanism behind the BR detection using GQDs.

To assess the potential for PET between GQDs and BR, UPS study was conducted to determine the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels of GQDs. The UPS spectra of GQDs (Fig. S8b) were analyzed where secondary electron cutoff (Ecutoff) was estimated at 15.81 eV and valence band onset (Eonset) at 1.26 eV, as illustrated in Fig. S8c and Fig.S8d. Additionally, Tauc plot was generated from UV–Vis absorption spectra to characterize the bandgap of GQDs, revealing it to be 3.06 eV (Fig. S9). The HOMO and LUMO energy levels were estimated to be −6.66 eV and −3.59 eV, respectively (detailed calculations are provided in ESI). Furthermore, the HOMO and LUMO levels of BR were found to be −4.94 eV and −3.02 eV, respectively, as reported [67]. Based on these results, an energy level diagram was constructed to represent the HOMO-LUMO levels of GQDs and BR (Fig. 3e). The LUMO energy level difference between GQDs and BR suggests that electron transfer from GQDs to BR is not feasible, given the significantly higher energy of the BR's LUMO level compared to that of GQDs. All these findings primarily support the conclusion that the fluorescence quenching of GQDs upon the addition of BR is primary attributed to inner filter effect.

Additionally, we compared the performance of our sensor system with other reported BR detection methods, such as HPLC, electrochemical, SERS and piezoelectric detection strategies, as shown in Table 1 . The concentration range and limit of detection of our sensor system are comparable to other reported methodologies and even superior in terms of rapid response, affordability, and simplicity. We have also highlighted various reports specifically focused on fluorescence-based sensor systems for BR detection in table S2 (ESI). Our sensor system, utilizing non-enzymatic, label-free and, undoped GQDs, demonstrates performance comparable to most of the reported fluorescence-based detection platforms, exhibiting excellent sensitivity towards BR. Significant spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of bilirubin and the PL spectra of GQDs amplified GQD's response towards BR. Furthermore, the optimization of critical reaction parameters such as pH, concentration of GQDs and reaction time further enhanced the sensor's performance, achieving a LoD of 38.96 nM.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed sensor system, a range of analytes were selected, and fluorescence detection strategy was implemented. GQDs were tested for sensory response towards various biomolecules, such as creatinine (cre), dopamine (dop), glucose (glu), urea (ure), l-cysteine (L-cy), l-Glutathione (L-Gl), Bovine serum Albumin (BSA), Hemoglobin (Hem), Uric Acid (UrA), Ascorbic Acid (AsA), l-arginine (L-ar), l-tyrosine (L-ty), Aspartic acid (ApA) and sucrose (Suc), along with BR as shown in Fig. 4 a. The concentration of BR was fixed at 6 μM, while for all other biomolecules, it was set to be 20 μM. Notably, negligible PL response was observed for any analyte other than BR. Beyond biomolecules, the selectivity of GQDs against common interfering ions, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Br−, Cl−, SO4 2− and CO3 2− along with BR was assessed, revealing high selectivity solely towards BR (Fig. 4b). Here also, the concentration of BR was maintained at 6 μM, while for all ions, it was fixed at 20 μM. Furthermore, the interference of these analytes in the presence of BR were also investigated (Fig. 4c and d), confirming negligible interference from either biomolecules or ions towards BR detection using GQDs. This study underscores the exceptional selectivity of GQDs towards BR in the presence of various interfering biomolecules and ions.

To assess the biocompatibility of the GQDs, cytotoxicity testing was conducted using MTT assay on L-132 cells. The L-132 cells were first incubated with 30, 60, 90 and 120 μg/mL concentrations of GQDs in 200 μL media, respectively. Subsequent analysis revealed that GQDs doesn't have any cytotoxic effect on the cell lines, as no significant cell death was observed (Fig. 4e). This result proves excellent biocompatibility of GQDs, affirming their applicability for detection of biomolecules.

The determination of BR concentration in blood plays a crucial role in the clinical diagnosis of liver-related disorders. Moreover, in severe cases, the analysis of urine samples offers a more convenient and noninvasive alternative. To validate the applicability of the developed sensor system in real world scenarios, real human samples (both blood and urine) were obtained from three healthy donors. The blood serum samples, with a known concentration of BR determined via the clinical Jendrassik and Grof method [69], were diluted and directly subjected to testing using GQDs through proposed spectroscopic method. The results, outlined in Table 2 , show comparable outcomes with conventional laboratory-scale Jendrassik and Grof method, exhibiting a maximum relative error of less than 6 %.

The urine sample, with an initially known BR concentration, was spiked with BR as mentioned in the assay preparation. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectrum was measured to obtain the results. The amount of BR in the samples were determined using the calibration plot (Fig. 2a). As evident from Table 3 , the determined BR concentration closely corresponds to the spiked concentrations, with robust recovery rates within the range of 94–103 %. Detailed explanations regarding the calculation of recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) can be found in the ESI. This investigation in real samples demonstrates the reliability of our sensor system in detecting BR in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D-printed POC device for detection of BR
ce:section.ce:para: After successfully evaluating the sensitivity and selectivity of GQDs towards BR, the next crucial step is to adapt the sensor system for point-of-care (POC) settings. Currently, detecting BR using the developed system heavily relies on spectrometers, laboratory-based consumables, and trained personnel. Since BR serves as a vital marker for liver function, there is a huge requirement for the development of compact, portable, and affordable technology to ensure accessibility to the public. In this regard, we have developed a compact, 3D-printed BR detection platform compatible with smartphones, capable of analyzing BR concentration through image analysis in a POC setup. The development process of BR detection platform mainly involved three key stages: first, developing the 3D-printed platform, followed by the fabrication of paper microfluidic devices for GQD-BR sampling, and finally, developing Fluoroimage for predicting BR concentration through fluorescence image analysis.

The disassembled view of the 3D-printed device platform, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a, comprises seven parts that can be assembled to form the complete device. It includes a smartphone holder (part 1) integrated with a camera focusing tube (part 3), with an optical filter (part 2) positioned between them to block the excitation light. The design of the smartphone holder-camera focusing tube system is compatible with any smartphone, regardless of the back camera's position. A rectangular base supports the smartphone holder, ensuring stability on a flat surface. A pivotal part of the platform is the cylindrical case, consisting of top and bottom sections (parts 4 and 6) which can be fixed together using mechanical locks. The cylindrical case facilitates the placement of a fidget spinner on its bottom section, along with a knob (part 5) featuring two legs that can rotate the spinner (refer Fig. S10). Positioned atop the top case is a SMD LED having excitation wavelength of 368 nm (emission spectra shown in Fig. S11), installed so that when the top part locks with the bottom part, the excitation light precisely illuminates the specific fidget spinner head focused by the camera. The top part of the case features two circular openings: one for accommodating the knob that rotates the fidget spinner and another for connecting the camera focusing tube (refer Fig. S12). The circular opening on the bottom part is used for connecting the supporting stage (part 7), which holds the whole device platform. Fig. 5b demonstrates the assembled configuration of the device platform, with all seven parts interconnected. The SMD LED was powered using an external 3.3 V supply provided by an Arduino uno board. The entire device platform is designed to replicate a completely dark environment within the cylindrical case once assembled, ensuring minimal interference from ambient light.

The sampling process involving GQDs and various concentrations of BR, was conducted using paper microfluidic devices that served as test strips. A single stamping was able to imprint a pattern onto Whatman filter paper, generating 37 devices, each with a circular active area measuring 8 mm in diameter. The complete fabrication protocol for producing these paper devices is shown in Fig. S12. After which, 5 μL of GQDs were drop-cast onto each paper device and dried at 50 °C for 10 min to achieve GQDs functionalization. The devices were then stored in a dry environment until further use. Fluoroimage then inputs the captured image, converting it into the RGB color space, which are then converted into grayscale values representing the fluorescence intensity of each image [70]. The application was initially calibrated using multiple paper devices functionalized with GQDs and incorporating various concentrations of BR, ranging from 0.25 μM to 14.50 μM.

To perform BR detection through image analysis, paper devices functionalized with GQDs were placed onto the fidget spinner heads, with the rotating knob attached and cylindrical case closed. BR samples were then dropped onto the paper devices through the second circular opening and allowed to react for 3 min before further processing. Fig. 5c shows the fully assembled device platform with six paper devices: one control (containing only GQDs) and five samples with varying concentration of BR, all placed on the fidget spinner heads. To capture the fluorescence of samples, a smartphone was placed onto the holder and swiftly captured images of the samples through the Fluoroimage app while rotating the fidget spinner. The specification of the smartphone camera sensor and additional parameters set during the capture are comprehensively detailed in the ESI. Fig. 5d demonstrates the operation of the entire device, with the smartphone camera focused on the sample. Fig. 5e illustrates the operational principle of the device platform, highlighting its key components.

For device calibration, we initially selected twelve paper devices with BR concentrations ranging from 0.25 μM to 14.50 μM, along with a sample containing only GQDs, covering the entire BR detection range used for spectrometer-based studies. Upon uploading the images, the Fluoroimage app provided grayscale values corresponding to each sample. As previously mentioned, the grayscale values of each image were considered to represent the PL intensity of the sample. Fig. 6 a displays a sequence of images captured by the smartphone camera using the device platform under SMD LED illumination. The first image shows the control sample, representing the paper device with only GQD functionalization, while the subsequent images show samples with increasing concentrations of BR. The optimal smartphone camera parameters set for capturing the images are explained in detail in the ESI.

The fluorescence quenching of GQDs is evident from the images, as the emission intensity decreases with increasing BR concentration. The sensory response, analyzed through image analysis, was determined by plotting the quenching efficiency (Ao-A)/Ao against BR concentration (Fig. 6b). Here Ao and A represent the grayscale value of the control and grayscale value corresponding to each sample, respectively. It is observed that the developed platform demonstrated the ability to detect BR within two consecutive linear ranges. The quenching efficiency exhibited a linear relationship with BR concentration from 0.00 μM to 4.00 μM following a fit equation y = 0.12562x + 2.6629 × 10−4, with a correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.965 Similarly, for the concentration range of 4.00 μM–14.50 μM, the response was fitted with a linear equation y = 0.02405x + 0.41775, with R2 = 0.972. Here y represents quenching efficiency (Ao-A)/Ao and x stands for concentration of BR in μM. Fig. 6c illustrates the highly linear response of the developed system within the lower BR concentration range of 0.00 μM–1.60 μM with an R2 value of 0.991. Utilizing these parameters, the detection limit was calculated to be 114.66 nM, highlighting the potential of the POC device for BR detection. The detailed calculation of the detection limit is elaborated in the ESI. Thus, the developed detection platform demonstrates significant potential for BR detection over a wide range of 0.25 μM–14.50 μM across two linear ranges. The smartphone application was calibrated using these linear ranges, and depending on the quenching efficiency of samples, the application selects the appropriate calibration equation for the range and calculates the BR concentration accordingly.

Following the calibration process, Fig. 7 illustrates the workflow of the Fluoroimage app, comprising of three main operational windows. In the first window, the user inputs the number of samples (up to a maximum of 5), followed by a second window for capturing and uploading the control image and the sample images. The final window displays BR concentration in each sample after image analysis, along with the grayscale value of each image for further analysis. The POC device thus demonstrated itself as an outstanding platform for fluorescence-based BR detection, without the need for complex instrumentation or specialized training. The performance of the developed POC detection platform is compared to various previously reported strategies that focused on smartphone-integrated systems for BR analysis and quantification based on digital image analysis, as shown in Table 4 . It was observed that the developed platform provided the highest sensitivity towards point-of-care BR detection compared to other reported studies. In addition to the sensitivity, this BR detection platform is compact, affordable and offers rapid operation with minimal analysis time.

In summary, this study successfully demonstrated the applicability of undoped, label-free graphene quantum dots for efficient BR detection. The GQDs exhibited blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 12.7 %, excellent photostability and biocompatibility, making them ideal for BR detection. BR effectively quenched the fluorescence of GQDs, with the Inner filter identified as the primary mechanism, achieving a LoD of 38.96 nM over a linear range of 0.18 μM–14.29 μM. Testing on real human serums showed promising results, comparable to the standard Jendrassik and Grof method used in clinical settings for BR detection. To enable point of care detection, a 3D-printed fluorescence image analysis platform was developed, incorporating an SMD LED excitation source and paper microfluidic devices for sampling. These samples were analyzed through an in-house developed smartphone application and quantified the BR concentration achieving an LoD of 114.66 nM. This compact and affordable detection platform offers a reliable and efficient approach to detect BR at POC setups, eliminating the need for complex instrumentation and trained manpower. Thus, it holds great potential for BR monitoring in resource limited settings.
